prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 5 minutes long when read aloud.

## Goal
Title: Separation anxiety at preschool drop-off
Description: Help Emma feel safe and confident when saying goodbye at preschool.

## Person
This is for Emma (age 4).
Developmental Tier: EARLY_CHILDHOOD

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Parent-child attachment and separation anxiety in early childhood" (2021)
   Key findings: Secure attachment reduces separation anxiety; predictable goodbye rituals lower cortisol
   Therapeutic techniques: Brief predictable goodbye ritual; reassurance statements; transitional object use

2. "Play-based CBT for preschool anxiety" (2022)
   Key findings: Puppet-mediated exposure reduces avoidance in 4-year-olds; parent coaching effective
   Therapeutic techniques: Gradual exposure; brave-kid praise; simple breathing (balloon breath)

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- Personalize for Emma (age 4) (developmental tier: EARLY_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 5 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as it will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine.
script:
Hello Emma. Welcome. I’m so glad you’re here with me. I know sometimes saying goodbye at preschool can feel a little scary. That’s okay. Lots of kids feel that way. Today, we’re going to practice being a brave kid and learn about a very special goodbye. Are you ready? Let’s begin.

First, let’s understand. You know what? Feeling a little miss for your grown-up is a sign of how much you love them. Your heart is just saying, I love you. And that’s a good heart. But your preschool is also a safe and fun place. Your grown-up will always come back. We can make the goodbye feel safer and shorter with a special plan. A plan just for you and your grown-up.

Now, let’s practice. We’ll start with a cozy breath. It’s called balloon breath. Put your hands on your tummy. Feel it right there. Now, take a slow breath in through your nose… like you’re filling a big, colorful balloon in your belly. Feel your hands move out. Good. Now let the air out slowly through your mouth… whoosh… like the balloon is gently floating away. Let’s do that two more times. Breathe in… fill the balloon. And breathe out… let it float. One more time. In… and out. Wonderful. You can do this balloon breath anytime you need to feel calm.

Next, let’s make a super fun goodbye plan. This is your special goodbye ritual. It’s like a secret handshake. It’s always the same, so you know exactly what will happen. Maybe it’s a big hug, then a high-five, then a silly wave at the window. You and your grown-up can decide. Let’s pretend right now. Imagine your grown-up is here. First, a big, big hug. Squeeze your arms around yourself. Feel that love. Then, a high-five. Slap your own hand. Pop! Then, you turn and see your teacher. You give your grown-up one last silly wave. And they wave back. You did it. That was your special goodbye.

Now, what makes you feel brave? Do you have a special stuffed friend or a cozy scarf? That’s called a transitional object. It’s a helper. It can come with you to preschool. It holds the love from home. You can give it a little squeeze if you need to remember that love. Imagine holding your helper now. Give it a little squeeze. Feel how brave you are.

You are such a brave kid. Practicing your special goodbye and your balloon breath helps your brain and your heart feel safer. Remember, your grown-up always comes back. Every single time.

You can practice your special goodbye with your grown-up at home. Make it fun. You can even use a puppet or a teddy bear to practice saying goodbye. The more you practice, the stronger your brave feelings get.

You did such good work today, Emma. I am so proud of you for learning about being brave. Keep your special goodbye plan and your balloon breath in your pocket for preschool. And remember, if those worried feelings feel too big, you can always ask your grown-up or your teacher for a little extra help. They are there for you. You’ve got this, brave kid. Goodbye for now.
